plan: Sell both leveraged ETFs (SOXL, SPAL) immediately — daily decay and volatility are incompatible with capital preservation.
Trim SMH from 13% to 5% to cut semiconductor concentration; trim CRWD from 12% to 5% to respect single-name cap.
Raise QQQI to 25% for steady income distribution and lower portfolio beta.
Add slightly to SPY (35%) as the core anchor; trim QQQ modestly to 15% to avoid redundant tech overlap.
Freed-up capital (~20%) sits in cash, acting as a buffer against the flagged ±8.95% monthly swing and funding tight-stop re-entries only if scanner confirms.
conservative.read: This posture targets roughly 1–1.5% per month through income distributions and slow index drift, with a meaningfully smaller drawdown profile than your current book. The honest risk is that trimming winners like CRWD early and holding heavy cash means you underperform sharply in a strong tech rally — opportunity cost is the real cost here.

moves.note: Slight trim, free cash for buffer

plan: Sell SPAL outright — it has the worst recent performance (-13.72%) and smallest allocation, so cutting it is low-regret and frees clean cash.
Trim SOXL to 2% (from 6%) — keeps a small leveraged chip kicker but stops the daily decay from compounding against you at full weight.
Trim SMH to 8% and QQQI to 13% to fund the cash buffer (target ~10%) without over-selling quality positions.
Add CRWD to 15% — it is your only current holding showing strong momentum (+7.4%) and fits the balanced growth theme.
Watch PANW at the scanner entry 351.34–351.69 with stop 350.62; only open 5% if it triggers, keeping total new buys funded by trims already made.
balanced.read: Balanced mode targets roughly 2–2.5% per month by blending index drift, CRWD momentum, and a small leveraged chip position, while the 10% cash buffer cushions the ~-17.91% worst-month scenario. The main risk is that trimming SMH and SOXL right before a semiconductor rebound leaves you underweight the sector's recovery — the 2-sigma downside is still meaningful at this leverage level.

plan: Double SOXL to 10% and add SPAL to 4% — you are deliberately leaning into leveraged recovery plays; both need hard stops at recent lows and daily monitoring for decay drag.
Add SMH to 15% to reinforce the semiconductor thesis across leveraged and unleveraged exposure simultaneously.
Add CRWD to 15% — your strongest current performer; widened stop per scanner but still strict.
Open ARM at 7% (entry 336.80–337.13, stop 336.18, R=9.46) and PANW at 6% (entry 351.34–351.69, stop 350.62, R=6.01) — these are the two highest-rated scanner setups and both are ACCUMULATE signals.
Trim SPY to 20%, QQQ to 10%, and QQQI to 8% to fund the new positions; total new buys are funded by these trims, keeping a ~5% cash buffer for stop-loss cushion.
aggressive.read: Aggressive mode stretches for 4%+ months by concentrating in the highest-scanner-rated names and adding leveraged ETF weight, but your worst-month 2-sigma scenario gets materially worse than the current -17.91% — leveraged ETFs decay daily and can lose the majority of their value in a sustained down move. This mode demands strict stop execution on every position; if stops are not honored, losses can compound very fast and margin calls become a real risk.

moves.note: No distribution, high beta single name
Reduce vol, keep tiny sector nod

plan: Sell SOXL, SPAL, and CRWD entirely — none pay distributions and all three add volatility that works against a low-drawdown income strategy.
Raise QQQI to 35% as the core income engine; its covered-call overlay generates monthly distributions while capping some upside in strong rallies.
Anchor the remainder in SPY at 35% for broad market participation with the lowest single-name risk in the book.
Trim QQQ to 10% and SMH to 5% to maintain a small tech/chip presence without concentrated volatility.
Keep roughly 15% in cash — distributions are variable and NAV can still fall in a selloff, so the cash buffer protects against forced margin liquidation in a bad month.
income.read: Income mode targets roughly 1% per month primarily through QQQI's covered-call distributions plus modest index drift from SPY, with the lowest expected drawdown of the four modes. The honest risk is that QQQI's NAV can still decline in a broad selloff and distributions are not guaranteed — this is not equivalent to a savings account, and in a sharp tech sell-off the 35% SPY/10% QQQ base will still pull the book down.
